title: LLMOps & Evaluation
description: Prompt/version management, evaluation sets, groundedness, observability, cost, latency, drift, and feedback loops.

questions.title: What is LLMOps?
questions.scenario: A GenAI platform team runs evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows. A recent review found that teams ship prompt and model changes without knowing which behavior regressed; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for LLMOps measured by regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task.
questions.expectedAnswer: LLMOps is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from golden datasets, traces, prompt versions, model versions, human labels, and judge scores, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems. LLMOps addresses that risk through model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from golden datasets, traces, prompt versions, model versions, human labels, and judge scores. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: How do you build a golden evaluation dataset?

commonMistakes: Explaining LLMOps as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task.
Ignoring the main risk: fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that LLMOps improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying LLMOps, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A GenAI platform team applied LLMOps in evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows. The baseline suffered from fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, then evaluated the change against regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for LLMOps, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: How would you explain prompt regression testing in a LLMOps & Evaluation interview?
questions.scenario: A GenAI platform team runs evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows. A recent review found that teams ship prompt and model changes without knowing which behavior regressed; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for prompt regression testing measured by regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task.
questions.expectedAnswer: prompt regression testing is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In evaluation and observability layer for RAG, agents, and prompt workflows, the practical approach is clear instruction hierarchy, examples, schema constraints, validation, and regression tests. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from golden datasets, traces, prompt versions, model versions, human labels, and judge scores, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using JSON schema, function calling, Promptfoo, LangSmith, and prompt/version registries, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is prompt changes silently breaking output format or tool behavior; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use regression pass rate, groundedness, answer relevance, p95 latency, and cost per successful task, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production.
